summary: The vertebral column has four curvatures: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, and sacral kyphosis.
The vertebral column has three curvatures.
The vertebral column has five curvatures including a coccygeal curve.
Primary curvatures (thoracic and sacral) are present at birth; secondary curvatures (cervical and lumbar) develop postnatally.
All four curvatures are present at birth.
Secondary curvatures are thoracic and sacral, developing postnatally.
The intervertebral disc has a central nucleus pulposus (notochord remnant) surrounded by the annulus fibrosus.
The nucleus pulposus surrounds the annulus fibrosus.
The intervertebral disc is entirely cartilaginous with no distinct layers.
L4-L5 and L5-S1 discs account for 90% of symptomatic disc prolapses.
T12-L1 and L1-L2 discs account for most disc prolapses.
Cervical discs account for 90% of symptomatic disc prolapses.
Lumbar puncture is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 because the spinal cord ends at L1-L2.
Lumbar puncture is performed at L1-L2 where the cord ends.
Lumbar puncture can be performed at any level safely.
The ligamentum flavum connects adjacent laminae and produces a characteristic pop during lumbar puncture.
The anterior longitudinal ligament produces a pop during lumbar puncture.
The ligamentum flavum connects adjacent vertebral bodies.
Tuberculosis of the spine (Pott's disease) is the most common cause of spinal cord compression in India.
Disc prolapse is the most common cause of spinal cord compression in India.
Metastatic cancer is the most common cause of spinal cord compression in India.
Ankylosing spondylitis affects the sacroiliac joint and is associated with HLA-B27.
Ankylosing spondylitis affects the hip joint primarily and is associated with HLA-DR4.
Ankylosing spondylitis is not associated with any HLA antigen.
